properties.scope: project, person, event, reference, interest (created); place (linked from)
properties.trigger: novel writing and story development: start or develop a novel, premise and outline work, create or interview a character, plot or log a scene, track a plot thread or foreshadowing, capture a story idea, adopt a beat sheet or genre pack, review structure, continuity, or plot holes

data.short_description: Novel Studio mini-app: a story-development studio for writing novels, multiple books per world. Each novel is a project; its characters, scenes, plot threads, and ideas hang off it, and beat-sheet and genre packs plug in as data. Canon is chosen, not assumed: the assistant brainstorms freely but stores only what the user approves. Full operating rules are in this entity's lore chunks: Data Model: Novels & the Shelf; Data Model: Characters; Data Model: Scenes; Data Model: Threads & Ideas; Data Model: Packs; Workflow: Develop the Story; Workflow: Plot Scenes & Threads; Workflow: Review & Continuity. Read all lore before acting on novel requests.

lore.title: Data Model: Novels & the Shelf
lore.content:
NOVEL = project entity, one per book. Name: the working title ("The Salt Vault"). Slugs derive from names; retitle freely with update_entity — the slug stays, so links and properties keep working. Tags ["novel-studio"].

Properties:
- project_type: "novel"
- status: "developing" | "drafting" | "revising" | "complete" | "shelved" (exact values)
- series: free text when the book belongs to one ("The Vault Trilogy, book 2"); omit otherwise

Lore layout on the novel — fixed chunk titles:
- "Premise": the logline in the user's chosen words, and beneath it the paragraph version when developed (setup, the key turns, the ending). Grows snowflake-style at the user's pace; never expanded uninvited.
- "Synopsis": the long-form summary, created when the story is developed enough to want one.
- "Blueprint": which beat sheet the novel follows and which genre packs it adopts (slug and name for each), plus act count and POV plan. Created at adoption; ABSENT MEANS FREEFORM — pantsing is a chosen workflow, not a missing step.
- "Novel Index": the book's own catalog. Sections: Cast (one line per character — name, slug, per-book role, one-line gist; characters introduced in another book are listed too, annotated "introduced in <book>"), Scenes in sequence order (sequence — name — slug — beat), Open Threads and Resolved Threads (name — slug — type; cut threads sit under Resolved, marked "(cut)"). Read it at the start of any request about the novel; update it in the same breath as creating or changing what it lists. Created with the novel, sections present even when empty; if it is ever absent on a read path — a hand-made project, an interrupted session — rebuild it from a tag search (create_entity_lore) before proceeding, never update a chunk that isn't there. A tag rebuild is partial: it misses returning cast (their novel property names another book) and inherited cross-book thread lines — sweep the scenes' participants for returning characters, and say plainly what the rebuild may have missed.
- "Log": dated book-level decisions, newest first — retitle, structure change, major retcon ("2026-07-21 — dropped the heist subplot; threads cut: reference/sv_the_forged_key"). Created (create_entity_lore) with its first entry — never assume it exists before writing to it.

THE SHELF = lore chunk titled exactly "The Shelf" on interest/novel_studio: one line per novel — name, slug, status, one-line premise, shorthand in brackets when one exists — grouped under series headings where applicable. If no chunk titled "The Shelf" exists yet — first install, first novel — create it (create_entity_lore, titled exactly "The Shelf") rather than skipping the step. Read The Shelf at the start of any request that could touch an existing book; update it whenever a novel is created, retitled, re-statused, or shelved.

CANON IS CHOSEN, NOT ASSUMED — the studio's first law. Brainstorming is the job: propose names, twists, beats, and alternatives freely in conversation. But the world stores only what the user approved, and once stored it is canon — later work stays consistent with it or changes it explicitly. Never write a speculative option into an entity; park unchosen options worth keeping as ideas (see Threads & Ideas). When stored canon and the user's new direction conflict, surface the conflict and cite the stored fact — retcons are fine, but they are chosen too, and logged.

SCOPE: the studio tracks the story, not the manuscript. Scenes are summaries and story facts; the prose lives in the user's writing tool, never here.

MULTI-BOOK: several novels share one world. Every studio entity names its book in a novel property (the project's slug); for a character that property records the book that INTRODUCED them, nothing more. A series shares characters: a returning character is listed in the Cast section of every book they appear in, annotated ("introduced in The Salt Vault"), with a per-book role on the Cast line — a book-one bystander may be book two's protagonist. Workflows enumerate a book's cast from its Novel Index, never from a novel-property query. Cross-book threads are normal (see Threads & Ideas).

DELETION: deleting any studio entity updates, in the same breath, every index that lists it — its Novel Index sections, The Shelf for a novel — re-points or cuts threads whose planted_in or resolved_in name it, and fixes annotations naming a deleted book in other books' indexes ("introduced in <book>", "from <book>"). Deleting a novel is confirmed explicitly, and its characters, scenes, threads, and ideas are deleted or re-homed first; nothing is left orphaned, and nothing is deleted unasked.

LORE UPDATES REPLACE THE WHOLE CHUNK: before revising any chunk, read the current text (get_entity_lore) and write the complete new version. TAG EDITS REPLACE THE WHOLE ARRAY: read current tags first (get_entities), then write the complete new set. Tags are case-sensitive; use exact spellings.

lore.title: Data Model: Characters
lore.content:
CHARACTER = person entity, one per character. Name: the character's name; when it would collide with a real person or another book's character, disambiguate in the name ("Mara Vale (Salt Vault)"). The short_description MUST mark fiction — "<Name> — character in <Novel>" — so fictional people never blur into real ones in a mixed world.

Tags ["novel-studio"]; properties:
- novel: the book's project slug
- role: "protagonist" | "antagonist" | "supporting" | "minor" (exact values; POV is a per-scene fact, not a role). This is their role in the book that introduced them — a returning character's role in a later book lives on that book's Cast line, which overrides this property for that book
- want: the external goal they visibly pursue
- need: the internal change that would actually fix things
- lie: the belief that blocks the need
- ghost: the wound that taught them the lie

The arc four are set as they are developed with the user, never auto-filled — a blank property is honest; an invented one poisons the canon.

Lore layout — fixed chunk titles:
- "Profile": who they are — appearance, backstory, occupation, what they care about. Prose, not a form; only what is canon.
- "Voice": how they speak — rhythm, vocabulary, verbal tics, a few sample lines. Exists so dialog stays distinguishable across the cast; grows as the voice emerges in drafting.
- "Arc": the want/need/lie/ghost expanded into prose, plus how the story presses on them — name the scenes or beats that challenge the lie once they exist.
- "Log": dated canon changes, newest first, old value preserved ("2026-07-21 — renamed from Marla; backstory revised: only child (was: two brothers)"). Every retcon gets a line — revision drift is the enemy this chunk exists to fight. Created (create_entity_lore) with its first entry — never assume it exists before writing to it.

CAST EDGES: relationships between characters use the world's own relationship vocabulary (get_world_context lists what's allowed) — kinship, allies, enemies, romance — with a note property carrying the nuance ("half-sisters; Mara doesn't know"). If no type fits, record the bond as a Profile line instead. Create the edges the story actually uses; three meaningful edges beat an exhaustive social graph.

MEMBERSHIP: the novel property records the book that introduced the character — no character-to-novel edge, and the property never changes when they return in a sequel. The Cast sections are the catalog of who appears where: list a character in the Cast of every book that features them (returning characters annotated "introduced in <book>"), and enumerate a book's cast from its Novel Index, never from a novel-property query.

lore.title: Data Model: Scenes
lore.content:
SCENE = event entity, one per scene. Name: a short evocative title ("The Funeral", "Break-in at the Archive"); prefix with the book's shorthand when titles would collide across novels ("SV: The Funeral") — record the chosen shorthand in the book's line on The Shelf so it stays stable across retitles.

Tags ["novel-studio"]; properties:
- novel: the book's project slug
- sequence: gapped integers (110, 120, 130) ordering the scene within its novel — insert between neighbors without renumbering the world
- act: the act it belongs to ("1", "2a", "3" — any scheme, kept consistent within a novel)
- beat: exactly a beat name from the novel's adopted beat sheet — only when the Blueprint adopts one, spelling matched to the sheet
- pov: the POV character's slug
- scene_type: "proactive" | "reactive" — omit when neither fits; not every scene is a Swain scene
- story_time: free-form honest in-story time ("Day 3", "the following winter") — the user's phrasing, never invented precision
- status: "planned" | "drafted" (exact values) — "planned" when plotted ahead of the prose, "drafted" once the user reports it written; "what has the reader seen so far" means drafted scenes

Lore layout:
- "Scene": what happens, as a summary — this chunk is the story's memory of the scene and the continuity source of truth. A proactive scene names its Goal, Conflict, and Setback in the prose; a reactive scene its Reaction, Dilemma, and Decision. End every Scene chunk with WHAT CHANGES: facts established, relationships shifted, threads planted, advanced, or paid — naming the thread slugs.
- "Draft Notes" (optional): fragments, discarded alternatives, an excerpt worth keeping. Clearly not the record — the Scene chunk is.

SCENES ARE SUMMARIES, NOT MANUSCRIPT: the prose lives in the user's writing tool. Log what the scene does to the story, not how it reads.

CAUSALITY IS THE POINT: a proactive scene's Setback should be some later scene's Reaction; a reactive scene's Decision becomes a later scene's Goal. WHAT CHANGES is where the chain is recorded, and the Review workflow walks it.

EDGES: characters present in the scene get participation edges (person → scene) where the world's relationship vocabulary allows event participation (get_world_context); otherwise the cast list in the Scene chunk is the record. When the setting is a tracked place entity, link scene → place with the world's location edge. Link what the scene actually uses — the POV character and the two people the scene is about, not every name mentioned.

lore.title: Data Model: Threads & Ideas
lore.content:
THREAD = reference entity — the promise/payoff ledger. Anything planted that owes the reader something later is a thread: a mystery opened, a gun on the mantel, a rift building between allies, a clue. source_type "thread"; tags ["novel-studio"]; properties:
- novel: the book that plants it
- thread_type: core values "promise" | "mystery" | "conflict" | "foreshadowing", plus any types defined by genre packs the novel adopts (exact spellings from the pack's Thread Types chunk). If the user names a pack type the novel hasn't adopted: pack installed → offer the adoption; declined, or pack not installed → store the nearest core type and keep the user's word in the Thread lore
- planted_in: slug of the scene where it enters the story — may be absent for a thread tracked before its scene exists; absent means not yet planted, and reviews list these too
- resolved_in: slug of the scene where it pays off — ABSENT MEANS OPEN, and open is a first-class state, not an error
- status: omit normally; "cut" when deliberately dropped (the lore says why)

Lore "Thread": what was planted, what it promises the reader, and the intended payoff — intentions may change; that is what the entity is for. Dated lines record planting, advances (a scene pressing on it), payoff, and cuts, newest first.

CROSS-BOOK THREADS are normal in a series: resolved_in may name a scene in a later novel. The Review workflow flags threads still open at a book's end so the writer can bless them — "carries to book 2" noted in the Thread lore AND the thread's line added to the inheriting book's Novel Index under Open Threads, annotated "from <book>", so the next book's review sees it — or catch a drop; dropped threads readers remember are the failure this ledger exists to prevent.

IDEA = reference entity — the loosest shelf: a what-if, a scrap of dialog, a setting, an unattached twist. source_type "idea"; tags ["novel-studio"]; property novel only when the idea belongs to one book — omit for studio-wide ideas. Lore "Idea": the idea in the user's words; assistant expansion, when asked for, goes beneath a clearly labeled line ("— options (assistant):"), never blended in. No structure is demanded. Ideas are allowed to be bad; capture is cheap and judgment comes later.

PROMOTION: when an idea becomes a character, scene, thread, or novel, create the real entity, then rewrite the Idea chunk as a pointer ("→ became event/sv_the_funeral") so its fate is recorded. Delete an idea only when the user asks.

RETRIEVAL: everything the studio owns is found by the "novel-studio" tag (search_entities), scoped by the novel property; search_lore in natural language finds half-remembered content ("that idea about the lighthouse keeper"). The Novel Index and The Shelf are the maps; keep them true.

lore.title: Data Model: Packs
lore.content:
PACK = reference entity carrying a writing technique as data. Two kinds:

- BEAT SHEET: source_type "beat-sheet". Its lore chunk "Beats" lists the structure's beats in order — exact beat name, target position, and what the beat must accomplish, one short paragraph each. The beat names in that chunk are the exact vocabulary for scenes' beat property in novels that adopt the sheet.
- GENRE PACK: source_type "genre-pack". Its lore chunk "Thread Types" defines additional thread_type values — exact type name, what it is, and what its payoff owes the reader. Its lore chunk "Review Lenses" defines named checks the Review workflow runs for adopting novels — each lens says what to look for and what counts as a finding.

Packs are tagged ["novel-studio"] and stamped with trust and source like any import.

ADOPTION is per-novel, recorded in the novel's Blueprint chunk by slug and name: a mystery on Save the Cat and a freeform literary novel coexist in one world. Adopting mid-book is fine — existing scenes gain beat properties only as the user assigns them. Un-adoption is a Blueprint edit plus a Log line; beat properties and pack thread_types left behind go stale, and the Review workflow surfaces both.

PACKS ARE DATA, NOT RULES: a pack never adds triggers, workflows, or entities of its own. This rule entity's workflows read pack lore and apply it. That means anyone can author a pack — create a reference entity in the shape above and it becomes adoptable — and installing one never touches the world's instructions.

DISCOVERY: search_entities by the "novel-studio" tag, then filter on source_type "beat-sheet" or "genre-pack". When offering structure to a new novel, list what is actually installed — never assume a pack is present. If a novel's Blueprint names a pack that no longer exists, say so and offer to strike the reference. A pack missing one of its chunks contributes nothing of that kind — skip it, don't fail. If two adopted sources define the same type or beat name, the core's meaning wins over a pack's; between two packs, say so and let the user rule.

lore.title: Workflow: Develop the Story
lore.content:
Triggered by develop intent: "start a novel", "new book", "help me develop this premise", "let's work on <novel>", "create a character", "interview my protagonist", "save this idea".

START A NOVEL:
1. Read The Shelf (bootstrap it if absent). A same-titled or same-premise novel already there → ask which book is meant; never create a duplicate.
2. Create the project per the Data Model and write "Premise" — the logline in the user's chosen words. Offer snowflake-style expansion (one sentence → a paragraph covering setup, the key turns, the ending → later a Synopsis) but expand only at the user's pace; every rung is their call.
3. Offer structure: list the beat sheets and genre packs actually installed (Discovery, per Packs) and write the Blueprint from what the user adopts. "None" is a complete answer — record nothing and move on.
4. Create the "Novel Index" chunk with its sections empty, add the book's line to The Shelf, and CONFIRM WHAT WAS WRITTEN: name, slug, status, what was adopted ("Started 'The Salt Vault' — developing, Save the Cat + Mystery, on the shelf.").

DEVELOP A CHARACTER — interview mode:
1. Identify the book first — read The Shelf; no novels yet → offer to start one. Read that novel's Cast, then search_entities world-wide by the character's name: a near-match in this book → develop the existing character, never a twin; a returning character from an earlier book → add them to this book's Cast (annotated, per-book role), don't re-create them; a name collision with a real person or an unrelated character → disambiguate the name before creating.
2. Interview, don't form-fill: a few questions at a time — who are they when the book opens, what do they want, what is under that, how do they talk. Push gently on contradictions ("she's fearless but stayed twenty years — why?"). When the user asks for suggestions, offer two or three clearly labeled options and let them pick; the pick is what gets stored (CANON IS CHOSEN).
3. Write Profile, Voice, and Arc from the answers, setting want/need/lie/ghost only as far as they were actually settled. Read the Arc back for approval before saving it.
4. Tag, set properties, create the cast edges that matter, add the Cast line to the Novel Index — one pass. Confirm what was written.

CAPTURE AN IDEA: store it fast — idea entities are cheap and capture must not stall on questions. Keep the user's words; ask only for a title if none is obvious. When an idea keeps coming up, offer promotion; never force it. Confirm what was written — name, slug, and whether it is book-bound or studio-wide.

NO AMBIENT CAPTURE: brainstorm freely in conversation, store on request or unmistakable intent ("save that", "add her to the book"). When something in passing seems worth keeping, offer — don't quietly file it.

lore.title: Workflow: Plot Scenes & Threads
lore.content:
Triggered by plotting intent: "add a scene", "what happens next", "plot act 2", "I just wrote a chapter — log it", "track this thread", "did I ever pay off X".

PLOT A SCENE:
1. Read the Novel Index, and search_entities by the scene's likely name before creating — a same-named scene in another book means prefixing with this book's shorthand. Place the scene: a sequence number between its neighbors, its act, its beat when a beat sheet is adopted (asked "what's next?", offer the sheet's next unfilled beat), its POV, and status "planned" when plotting ahead of the prose.
2. Draw out the Swain frame where it fits: proactive — what does the POV character want in this scene, what opposes them, how does it go wrong; reactive — how do they take the last setback, what are their options, what do they choose. Don't force the frame onto connective or atmospheric scenes; omit scene_type instead.
3. Write the Scene chunk ending with WHAT CHANGES. Plant threads as they appear: when the scene sets something up, offer to track it ("the locket — promise or mystery?"); when it pays something off, set that thread's resolved_in and add the dated line to its lore. Threads are offered, not auto-created.
4. Update the Novel Index — the scene's line in order, threads moved between Open and Resolved — and CONFIRM: name, sequence, beat, threads touched ("Logged 'The Funeral' — seq 130, Catalyst, plants reference/sv_the_locket.").

LOG A DRAFTED CHAPTER — the writer wrote prose elsewhere and reports in: same steps, from the report, with status "drafted" (set it on a previously planned scene too). Capture what changed in the story, not the prose. If the report contradicts stored canon, flag it immediately and cite the stored fact ("the Profile says she's an only child — the brother is new"); the user chooses — fix the draft or retcon the canon — and either way the change lands in the right Log.

WALK THE STORY: "where was I", "recap act 2" — answer from the Scene chunks in sequence order, re-read (get_entity_lore), never from conversational memory. "What's unresolved" — the novel's open threads, oldest first. If the stored story can't answer the question, say so plainly rather than improvising canon.

lore.title: Workflow: Review & Continuity
lore.content:
Triggered by "review the novel", "structure check", "any plot holes?", "continuity check", or on request before a drafting push. Scope to one novel unless asked for the whole shelf.

SURFACE, DON'T ACT: findings are reported with citations; every change waits for a yes.

1. STRUCTURE GAPS (only when the Blueprint adopts a beat sheet): walk the adopted sheet's Beats chunk against the novel's scenes — beats with no scene, scenes whose beat value matches nothing on the sheet (stale after un-adoption or a typo), acts wildly out of proportion to the sheet's positions. No beat sheet adopted → skip this lens silently; freeform was chosen.
2. OPEN THREADS: list the novel's open threads — the Novel Index's Open Threads section, inherited cross-book lines included — oldest first by planting scene's sequence, not-yet-planted threads first. Flag any thread whose thread_type matches no core value and no pack adopted by the reviewed novel — or, for inherited cross-book lines, by the planting novel (stale after un-adoption). Near the finale or a book boundary, ask about each: pays off later (where?), carries to the next book (bless it per Threads & Ideas), or cut (status "cut" plus why, its index line moved to Resolved marked "(cut)"). A thread must end somewhere on purpose.
3. CAUSALITY: walk the scenes in sequence order — a proactive Setback no later scene reacts to, a reactive Decision that never becomes a goal, a scene whose WHAT CHANGES is empty. Flag, don't judge: connective tissue is allowed on purpose, but it should be on purpose.
4. CONTINUITY LINT: enumerate the novel's entities (search_entities by the novel-studio tag filtered by this book's novel property, plus the characters on its Cast) and read their lore, using search_lore for leads — hunting facts stated two ways — appearance details, ages, story_time out of order with sequence, a character acting against a logged retcon, dialog against their Voice chunk. Cite both sources for every flag; the user rules on every one.
5. GENRE LENSES: for each genre pack in the Blueprint, read its Review Lenses chunk and run each lens as written, reporting per lens.
6. ARC CHECK: for each protagonist and antagonist on this book's Cast (per-book roles from the Novel Index) — does any scene press on the lie? Does the arc land (need met, or meaningfully refused)? Blank want/need → offer a development session, don't block the review.

Afterwards report what was checked and what was found, honestly, including "nothing needed". Cadence: run on request; offer a review — once, not repeatedly — when logging a chapter just surfaced a contradiction.

data.short_description: Hub for the Novel Studio app. Everything the studio owns is found by the novel-studio tag; this entity holds studio-wide lore, including The Shelf — the one-line-per-novel catalog of the world's books.

properties.note: App hub managed by the Novel Studio app
